title: ARC Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on Ways and Means, for a period to be
plain_summary: The ARC Act of 2025 helps people avoid losing their arms or legs because of a blood vessel disease called peripheral artery disease. The law makes Medicare and Medicaid pay for screening tests to find this disease early in people who might get it, and patients do not have to pay anything extra. The government will also create programs to teach people about this disease and set up special programs to help doctors prevent amputations. The goal is to catch the disease before it gets so bad that people need to have body parts removed.
last_action_text: Sponsor introductory remarks on measure. (CR H122)

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title; Findings
sections.text: This Act may be called the Amputation Reduction and Compassion Act of two thousand twenty five or the ARC Act of two thousand twenty five. Congress has found several important facts. When blood flow is reduced because arteries become narrowed or blocked with fatty deposits, this condition is called atherosclerosis. Atherosclerosis causes more deaths in the United States than any other condition. Heart attacks happen when the heart's arteries get clogged and are the leading cause of death in America. Atherosclerosis also happens in the legs and is called peripheral artery disease or PAD. Having PAD greatly increases the risk of heart attack, stroke, amputation, and death. While most Americans know about atherosclerosis in the heart, many Americans have never heard of PAD. Americans with PAD often do not know about the serious risks of the disease. An estimated twenty one million Americans have PAD. About two hundred thousand of them suffer preventable amputations every year because of this disease. This happens more often to minorities. According to the Dartmouth Atlas, amputation risks for African Americans living with diabetes are as much as four times higher than the national average. Data shows that Native Americans are more than twice as likely to have an amputation and Hispanics are up to seventy five percent more likely to have an amputation. Fifty two percent of patients with an above the knee amputation and thirty three percent of patients with a below the knee amputation will die within two years of their amputation. Screening and arterial testing for PAD is cost effective and should be part of routine medical care. Once PAD is found, amputations and deaths can be reduced by using national evidence based PAD care guidelines. Americans with a PAD diagnosis have a sixty seven percent increase in the risk of heart related death compared to people without a PAD diagnosis. Therefore, screening for PAD helps health care professionals identify heart risk factors earlier and take steps to reduce the risk of heart related death.

sections.title: Section 2  Peripheral Artery Disease Education Program
sections.text: This section adds a new part to the Public Health Service Act. The Secretary of Health and Human Services will work with the Director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. They will also work with the Administrator of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, the Administrator of the Health Resources and Services Administration, leading clinical and patient advocacy organizations, and other interested groups. Together they will create and coordinate a peripheral artery disease education program. This program will support, develop, and put in place educational efforts and outreach strategies. These will inform health care professionals and the public about peripheral artery disease and ways to reduce amputations related to this disease. Special attention will be given to at risk populations. The Secretary will identify and share with health care professionals the best practices for peripheral artery disease when appropriate. There is authorized to be appropriated six million dollars for each of fiscal years two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty to carry out this section.

sections.title: Section 3  Medicare Coverage of Peripheral Artery Disease Screening Tests Furnished to At-Risk Beneficiaries Without Imposition of Cost-Sharing Requirements
sections.text: This section changes the Social Security Act to add coverage for peripheral artery disease screening tests for at risk beneficiaries under Medicare. A peripheral artery disease screening test means noninvasive physiologic studies of extremity arteries which is commonly called ankle brachial index testing. It also means arterial duplex scans of lower extremity arteries vascular. The Secretary may determine other items and services that are appropriate for screening for peripheral artery disease for at risk beneficiaries after consulting with relevant stakeholders. An at risk beneficiary means an individual who is entitled to or enrolled for benefits under Medicare part A and enrolled for benefits under part B. This includes people who are sixty five years of age or older. It includes people who are at least fifty years of age but not older than sixty four years of age with risk factors for atherosclerosis such as diabetes mellitus, a history of smoking, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension or a family history of peripheral artery disease. It includes people who are younger than fifty years of age with diabetes mellitus and one additional risk factor for atherosclerosis. It includes people with a known atherosclerotic disease in another vascular bed such as coronary, carotid, subclavian, renal, or mesenteric artery stenosis, or abdominal aortic aneurysm. The Secretary will work with appropriate organizations to establish standards about how often peripheral artery disease screening tests should be done for coverage under Medicare. These screening tests will be included in the initial preventive physical examination. Medicare will pay one hundred percent of the actual charge for the services or the amount determined under the payment basis. No deductible will apply to these screening tests. These tests will be excluded from the prospective payment system for hospital outpatient department services. Medicare will not cover tests that are performed more frequently than allowed. The Secretary cannot modify or eliminate coverage of these preventive services. These changes will apply to items and services furnished on or after January first two thousand twenty six.

sections.title: Section 4  Medicaid Coverage of Peripheral Artery Disease Screening Tests Furnished to At-Risk Beneficiaries Without Imposition of Cost-Sharing Requirements
sections.text: This section changes the Social Security Act to add coverage for peripheral artery disease screening tests for at risk beneficiaries under Medicaid. A peripheral artery disease screening test means noninvasive physiologic studies of extremity arteries which is commonly called ankle brachial index testing. It also means arterial duplex scans of lower extremity arteries vascular. The Secretary may determine other items and services that are appropriate for screening for peripheral artery disease for at risk beneficiaries after consulting with relevant stakeholders. An at risk beneficiary under Medicaid means an individual enrolled under a State plan or a waiver of such plan. This includes people who are sixty five years of age or older. It includes people who are at least fifty years of age but not older than sixty four years of age with risk factors for atherosclerosis such as diabetes mellitus, a history of smoking, hyperlipidemia, and hypertension or a family history of peripheral artery disease. It includes people who are younger than fifty years of age with diabetes mellitus and one additional risk factor for atherosclerosis. It includes people with a known atherosclerotic disease in another vascular bed such as coronary, carotid, subclavian, renal, or mesenteric artery stenosis, or abdominal aortic aneurysm. The Secretary will work with appropriate organizations to establish standards about how often peripheral artery disease screening tests should be done for coverage under a State plan under Medicaid. No cost sharing will be required for these screening tests. This applies to regular cost sharing and alternative cost sharing arrangements.

sections.title: Section 5  Development and Implementation of Quality Measures
sections.text: The Secretary of Health and Human Services will work with relevant stakeholders to develop quality measures for nontraumatic lower limb major amputation. These measures will use appropriate diagnostic screening including peripheral artery disease screening. The goal is to encourage alternative treatments including revascularization instead of amputation. No later than eighteen months after this Act is enacted, the Secretary will complete appropriate testing and validation of these measures. The Secretary will then include these measures in quality reporting programs for appropriate providers of services and suppliers under the Medicare program. This includes the merit based incentive payment system, incentive payments for participation in eligible alternative payment models, the shared savings program, models under section eleven fifteen A of the Social Security Act, and other payment systems or models as the Secretary may specify.

sections.title: Section 6  Amputation Prevention Pilot Program
sections.text: This section changes the Social Security Act to add a new model for testing. The model will promote voluntary nontraumatic lower limb major amputation prevention programs at hospitals, ambulatory surgical centers, and office based centers. These programs will increase access to amputation prevention services, reduce amputation rates, and reduce costs to hospitals, surgical centers, and office based centers. The programs will do this through patient risk modification and management, early screening and detection and surveillance, testing and treatment for peripheral artery disease, and improved care coordination for individuals at high risk for amputation. No later than eighteen months after this Act is enacted, the Deputy Administrator and Director of the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation will test this model.
